text: During the laparoscopic low anterior resection, the surgeon uses a camera lens and two instruments to carefully dissect the lower colon or sigmoid. The left ureter is identified and marked to prevent injury. The mesorectum, containing regional lymph nodes, is dissected away from the pelvic bones, blood vessels, and nerves, to be removed with the rectal tumor.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter before dissecting the right side of the mesorectum. The end of the right fallopian tube is visible above the cautery instrument. The right hypogastric nerve trunk is identified and preserved, ensuring the patient's sexual function remains intact. The rectum is mobilized using an electrosurgical device, minimizing bleeding risk due to few blood vessels in the tissue plane. As dissection approaches the pelvic floor, the space around the rectum narrows, requiring careful attention to nearby blood vessels and organs such as the vagina and prostate gland.

The surgeon uses ultrasonic scissors to dissect and seal small blood vessels while mobilizing the rectum. A digital rectal exam is performed to locate the rectal tumor, followed by the use of a laparoscopic stapling device to divide the rectum below the tumor with two applications, sealing the bowel with titanium staples. The sigmoid colon is dissected and divided at a marked site, ensuring the removal of mesorectum lymph nodes. Large arteries and veins are individually dissected, clipped, and divided with ultrasonic scissors.

The surgeon uses ultrasonic scissors to divide small blood vessels near the bowel. The left lower quadrant laparoscopic incision is extended to four centimeters, and a wound protection sleeve is inserted to prevent tumor cell implantation during extraction. The rectal portion containing the tumor is removed and the bowel is divided, with the specimen sent for pathological examination.

The surgeon secures the anvil and resumes laparoscopy, ensuring the anvil reaches the remaining rectum. The end-to-end anastomotic stapler is inserted into the rectum, and the center spike shaft is advanced to the rectal stump near the staple line. The stapler is connected, closed, and fired, joining the bowel ends with two concentric circles of titanium staples and cutting a hole inside the ring. The device is opened and extracted, and the shaft is inspected for complete tissue rings from the sigmoid and rectum. A clamp is placed across the lower colon, the pelvis is filled with saline, and the proctoscope is inserted into the rectum. Air is introduced into the rectum, and the absence of bubbles in the saline confirms an airtight seal at the new bowel connection.
The surgeon suctions fluid from the pelvis using a small drain to prevent fluid buildup. Three small incisions are then closed with absorbable sutures, marking the completion of the operation.
